title: How to Use Somatic Practices for Financial Healing
body:
Your Body Holds Your Money Story: Somatic Healing for Financial Trauma

Truth is, you're probably broke, or feel broke, because your body thinks you're still running from a sabre-toothed tiger. No, seriously. Your financial stress isn't just in your head. It's in your gut. It's in your shoulders. It's a knot in your chest. You feel that? We talk a lot about mindset and money. We read books like Mind Over Money (paid link) and tell ourselves positive affirmations. We budget, we invest, we hustle. But for so many of us, the needle barely moves. Or it moves, and then we self-sabotage. We make a bunch of money, only to lose it. We get ahead, then something "unexpected" happens and we're back to square one. It's infuriating. It's exhausting. Why? Because your relationship with money isn't just a mental construct. It's deeply, viscerally wired into your nervous system. It's stored in your tissues, your breath, your posture. Money is just energy with a story attached to it. And that story, for most of us, is one of scarcity, fear, shame, and unworthiness. It's a story written by generations of ancestral trauma, childhood experiences, and societal conditioning. It's a story your body remembers, even if your conscious mind tries to deny it. Think about it. When you get a bill you can't pay, where do you feel it? Is it a tightening in your stomach? A racing heart? Shallow breath? That's your body's ancient survival response kicking in. Fight, flight, freeze. It doesn't differentiate between a literal physical threat and a perceived financial one. To your nervous system, a looming debt can feel just as dangerous as a predator. This isn't woo-woo. This is basic biology. Dr. Barbara Huson, author of Sacred Success (paid link), talks extensively about how women, in particular, carry financial trauma and how it manifests in their bodies and their inability to earn what they're worth. Geneen Roth, known for her work with emotional eating, also brilliantly connects our relationship with food to our relationship with money - both often being proxies for deeper emotional needs and traumas. We're talking about deeply ingrained patterns, not just poor choices. Your bank account is a mirror, not a scorecard. It reflects what's going on inside you, particularly in your nervous system. If you're constantly in a state of fight-or-flight around money, you're literally blocking the flow of abundance. You're creating an internal environment of scarcity, and the external world will reflect that right back to you. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. It's a state of being, not a number in a bank.

The Problem With "Just Think Positive"

You've tried positive affirmations, haven't you? You've repeated "I am wealthy" a thousand times while your stomach churned with anxiety. You've visualized abundance while your shoulders were hunched up to your ears. Did it work? Probably not, or not sustainably. Here's why: your conscious mind can say one thing, but your body, your subconscious, your nervous system, is screaming another. And guess who wins that battle every single time? The body. The subconscious. It's more powerful. It's primal. It's designed for survival. If your body believes, at a cellular level, that money is dangerous, or that having money means you'll be abandoned, or that you're not worthy of it, no amount of mental gymnastics will override that. You'll find ways to push money away, to spend it recklessly, to avoid looking at your statements, to stay in jobs that underpay you. You'll recreate your familiar financial story, even if it's painful. Know what I mean? This is where somatic practices come in. Somatic work isn't about thinking your way out of your money problems. It's about feeling your way through them. It's about bringing awareness to the physical sensations in your body, allowing them to be there, and gently, slowly, releasing the stored tension and trauma. It's about resetting your nervous system so it can finally feel safe around money.

What is Somatic Healing and Why Does it Matter for Money?

Somatic healing, at its core, is about connecting with your body's felt sense. It's about listening to the messages your body is constantly sending you, messages that often get drowned out by the chatter of your mind. It's based on the understanding that trauma - and financial trauma is absolutely trauma - isn't just a memory; it's a physical imprint. When you experience stress, especially chronic financial stress, your body goes into survival mode. Your sympathetic nervous system revs up. Your muscles tense. Your breath becomes shallow. Your digestion slows. This is great if you need to run from a tiger. It's terrible if you're trying to make rational financial decisions or attract abundance. Somatic practices help you regulate your nervous system. They teach you how to move out of that constant fight-flight-freeze state and into a more relaxed, present state where you can actually think clearly, feel safe, and allow new possibilities to emerge. Think of it like this: your body is a sophisticated antenna. If that antenna is full of static and interference (stress, trauma), it can't receive clear signals (opportunities, abundance). Somatic work helps clear the static. George Kinder, a pioneer in the financial life planning movement, understood that money isn't just about numbers; it's deeply tied to our deepest values, fears, and dreams. He encouraged clients to explore what truly matters to them, which often meant confronting uncomfortable truths about their relationship with money and their own mortality. This kind of deep self-inquiry naturally leads to somatic awareness, because those truths live in the body.

Practical Somatic Tools for Financial Healing

You don't need to become a yoga master or a meditation guru overnight. Somatic healing is about small, consistent practices that help you tune into your body.

1. The Body Scan for Money Stress

This is a foundational practice. Find a quiet place where you won't be disturbed. Lie down or sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Start by bringing your attention to your breath. Don't try to change it, just notice it. Is it shallow? Deep? Fast? Slow? Now, slowly move your attention through your body, starting from your toes and moving all the way up to the crown of your head. As you focus on each part - your feet, your calves, your thighs, your hips, your belly, your chest, your arms, your hands, your shoulders, your neck, your face, your head - ask yourself: "What sensations do I feel here?" Don't judge. Just observe. Is there tingling? Numbness? Tightness? Heaviness? Lightness? Pulsing? Heat? Cold? When you encounter a sensation, especially one related to money stress (a knot in your stomach when thinking about bills, tension in your jaw when contemplating your budget), don't try to fix it. Just acknowledge it. "Ah, there's tightness in my chest when I think about my debt." Breathe into that area. Imagine your breath creating space around the sensation. Stay with it for a few moments. Sometimes, just acknowledging it without judgment is enough to start the release. Does that land? Do this for 5-10 minutes daily, especially when you're feeling financially triggered or before making a money decision.

2. Grounding Practices

When your nervous system is dysregulated, you feel "ungrounded" - scattered, anxious, disconnected. Grounding practices help bring you back into your body and the present moment, creating a sense of stability. * **Feel Your Feet:** Stand up. Really feel your feet on the floor. Wiggle your toes. Shift your weight from side to side. Imagine roots growing from the soles of your feet deep into the earth. Feel the solidity beneath you. * **The 5-4-3-2-1 Technique:** This is a quick way to bring your senses into the present. * Name 5 things you can see. * Name 4 things you can feel (the chair beneath you, your clothes, the air). * Name 3 things you can hear. * Name 2 things you can smell. * Name 1 thing you can taste. * **Deep Belly Breathing:** Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Inhale slowly through your nose, feeling your belly rise. Exhale slowly through your mouth, feeling your belly fall. Make your exhales slightly longer than your inhales. This activates your parasympathetic nervous system, the "rest and digest" system. Practicing these throughout your day, especially when you feel financial anxiety creeping in, can make a huge difference.

3. Movement and Tremoring

Our bodies are designed to move. When animals in the wild experience trauma (like being chased by a predator), they often shake or tremor afterwards to release the stored energy. Humans, with our big brains, often override this natural mechanism, leading to chronic tension. * **Gentle Shaking:** Stand with your knees slightly bent. Gently shake your entire body - your legs, your arms, your torso, your head. Just let it be loose and free. Do this for a few minutes. You might notice a tingling sensation or even emotional release. * **Yoga or Qigong:** These ancient practices are inherently somatic. They connect breath, movement, and awareness, helping to release tension and improve energy flow. You don't need to be an expert; even a few simple stretches or mindful movements can be powerful. * **Dance:** Put on some music that moves you and just let your body express itself. No choreography, no judgment. Just pure, uninhibited movement. Remember, the goal isn't to "fix" anything immediately. It's to create a container of safety within your body where these old, stuck energies can begin to move and release.

Releasing Financial Trauma from the Nervous System

This isn't a quick fix. It's a journey. But it's one that leads to real and lasting change. When you consistently engage in somatic practices, you're essentially retraining your nervous system. You're teaching it that it's safe to relax, safe to receive, safe to have money. Krishnamurti, the philosopher and spiritual teacher, spoke extensively about the importance of observing without judgment. This non-judgmental awareness is key in somatic work. When you observe your body's sensations around money without labeling them "good" or "bad," you create space for them to simply be, and in that space, they can begin to dissipate. Think about times you've self-sabotaged financially. Maybe you got a raise and immediately spent it all. Or you avoided looking at your investment statements. Or you defaulted on a loan. These aren't just "bad habits." They're often unconscious attempts to return to a familiar state, even if that state is scarcity. Your nervous system prefers the familiar, even if it's painful, over the unknown, even if the unknown promises growth and abundance. By working somatically, you're creating a new familiar. A familiar that feels safe and regulated, even when money is present. You're building your capacity to hold more, to receive more, without your nervous system going into a panic. It also means dealing with the uncomfortable emotions that come up. Shame. Fear. Guilt. Anger. These emotions are often trapped in the body. When you allow yourself to feel them, truly feel them, without judgment, they can begin to move through you. This is why some people find somatic work challenging at first. It asks you to lean into discomfort, but it's discomfort with a purpose: release. This process is what allows you to truly integrate practices like those found in You Are a Badass at Making Money (paid link). It's not enough to intellectually understand that you're capable of making money. Your body has to *believe* it, has to *feel* it as a safe and natural state.

Integrating Somatics into Your Financial Life

Somatic work isn't just something you do on a meditation cushion. It's a way of being, a way of approaching your entire life, including your finances. * **Before Financial Decisions:** Before you make a big purchase, invest, or even just pay a bill, pause. Do a quick body scan. How do you feel? Is your nervous system calm or agitated? If you're agitated, take a few minutes to ground yourself before proceeding. This can prevent impulsive, fear-driven decisions. * **When Reviewing Statements:** This is a big one for many. Instead of dreading it, approach it with curiosity and compassion. As you look at your bank account or credit card statement, notice what sensations arise in your body. Breathe into them. Acknowledge any fear or shame without letting it overwhelm you. * **Celebrating Wins:** When you achieve a financial goal, big or small, don't just move on to the next thing. Pause. Feel the joy, the relief, the pride in your body. Let those positive sensations fully land and integrate. This helps rewire your nervous system to associate money with positive experiences. * **Addressing Underearning:** Barbara Huson's work on underearning is so relevant here. Many people, particularly women, unconsciously block themselves from earning more due to deep-seated beliefs about worthiness, safety, or cultural conditioning. Somatic practices can help uncover these unconscious blocks and release the physical tension associated with them, allowing you to finally step into your full earning potential. This isn't about ignoring your financial reality. It's about changing your *relationship* to that reality. It's about creating an internal environment where you can respond to challenges from a place of calm and clarity, rather than panic and reaction.

FAQ

What if I don't feel anything during a body scan?

That's completely normal, especially at first. Many of us are so disconnected from our bodies that we don't immediately feel sensations. Don't judge yourself. Just keep practicing. The goal isn't to "find" something, but to bring gentle attention. Over time, as you build awareness, you'll start to notice subtle shifts.

Is somatic healing a substitute for financial planning or therapy?

Absolutely not. Somatic healing is a powerful *complement* to traditional financial planning and psychological therapy. Financial planning gives you the practical tools and strategies. Therapy can help you process cognitive patterns and past experiences. Somatic work addresses the deeper, embodied trauma that often underlies financial dysfunction. They all work best together.

How long does it take to see results from somatic practices for money?

There's no fixed timeline. Some people feel shifts almost immediately, experiencing a sense of calm or release. For others, it's a gradual process of consistent practice. The key is consistency and compassion. You're rewiring decades of conditioning, so be patient with yourself. Every small step toward embodied awareness is a win.

Can somatic work really help with actual money problems, like debt?

Yes, indirectly but powerfully. Somatic work won't magically pay off your debt, but it will change your internal state. When you're less anxious and more grounded, you're better able to think clearly, make rational decisions, seek out resources, and take consistent action without self-sabotage. It helps you get out of the "freeze" response that keeps you stuck and into a more resourceful state.

Look, your financial journey is intimately connected to your physical and emotional well-being. You can't separate your money from your body. Start listening to what your body is telling you. It holds the keys to your financial freedom. Today, right now, take five minutes. Close your eyes. Place a hand on your heart and a hand on your belly. Take three slow, deep breaths. Just notice. No judgment. Just presence. That's your first step. Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Heal your financial stress. Learn how somatic practices can release financial trauma stored in your body. Discover practical steps for financial well-being.
author: Kalesh
